prompt: Preach on the princes and the poor. What is the condition of the common folk under the rule of the great lords?
text: I will teach you what condition they are in. They suffer such oppression that it cannot be described except by crying to God from suffering heart: 'O Lord!' For the good man does not know how much his soul suffers when he sees his brother oppressed and cruelly treated while he himself is required to pay taxes for the protection of tyranny, which does nothing but harm to the poor. Therefore I say this also to you, who have been so long in your comfortable situation that you do not know what is happening in the world: The common man has lost all fear before his lord and oppressor, because he sees that his lord kills the innocent without mercy. For if you kill a man of power or wealth — then the whole community rises against you with great outcry, as you know from Luther's preaching. But if you kill a poor man who has done nothing wrong — then the whole community is satisfied and praises God for your deed, because it sees what an oppressive burden this man was! And so the lords have no fear of killing their subjects, because they can always count on the approval of the common people. This is the condition in which most Christians live today: They are oppressed by the powerful and flatter those who oppress them, out of a mixture of cowardice, stupidity, and false piety. This cannot last much longer. The spirit of God is moving through the world as it has never moved before, calling people to awake from their sleep and take action for justice.

prompt: What does the present age demand of those who call themselves Christian?
text:
The whole world is sick and full of sorrow. Its root is in human pride, which has become so powerful that it can no longer be restrained by fear or hope. The godless tyrants who have arisen from this root are too strong for the weak to resist with prayers alone. They must be resisted with all possible force — not because violence justifies itself, but because those who use it do so in obedience to God's command and to protect the oppressed from oppression.

Therefore, I tell you: Whoever fears death more than he fears God has no true faith. For when Christ was given over to death by the enemies of righteousness, he did not pray that they would cease persecuting him with violence but said instead, 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.' This is a sign that those who truly fear God will not shrink from suffering and even dying at their hands if this is required in order to carry out the will of God. But those who flee such sufferings are not truly followers of Christ; they are merely people who use Christianity as a cover for their own cowardice.

This is why I have preached to the common people that they should arm themselves and stand fast against the powerful oppressors, knowing that in doing so they are not serving their own interests but simply obeying the command of God.

prompt: Who are the Elect? And who are the godless?
text:
The Elect are those for whom Christ is not just a name but the very substance. They hear and embrace his word as living fire that burns through all pretense to destroy everything that would stand between them and their creator. The elect feel his presence in themselves in every fiber of their being — from the tips of their toes to the furthest reaches of their minds. And they know, beyond any doubt, that this experience is not merely a subjective feeling but an objective reality, confirmed by the entire history of the prophets and apostles, by the testimony of every truly holy person who ever lived, and by the unerring evidence of their own hearts.

The elect walk in the path of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, David, Elijah, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Daniel, John the Baptist, Jesus Christ, and his apostles. They follow them not just with their minds but with their whole beings — with their desires, fears, hopes, doubts, sorrows, joys, every trembling and whispering at the edge of consciousness. To walk thus is to be in constant conflict with the godless — with those who seek to comfort themselves by telling themselves pleasant lies about God's approval instead of facing him in his true nakedness as revealed through Christ. The elect know that it would be easier to build a castle impregnable against all earthly enemies than to stand firm against the complacency, the self-deception, the sheer unwillingness to look at reality head-on that characterizes most people.

prompt: Preach against the merchants and the usurers.
text: The usurious trade is not only a sin against God: it is also a sin of violence — a form of theft by fraud through which the poor man loses his last goods and finally all hope; yes, for many he falls into despair so deep that he cannot rise again from it. For the whole structure of this world was built up precisely on such despair: the rich on top, the deceived below in suffering and death — and they know nothing about their condition except that they are poor and oppressed, helpless before those who rule them. This is not a human arrangement but the direct work of the devil himself for his own purposes, which are to keep God's people divided against themselves so that he may continue to mock their faith with mocking successes, victories without victory in the only sense that matters, where the spirit of God is present and works among men as it did at Pentecost. Therefore I urge you: preach against usury with all your strength — not because usurers are terrible sinners who must be punished, but because they are instruments of the devil to keep Christless a people Christless in every generation until he comes again in fire and blood to judge them along with their fathers.

prompt: What does the gospel truly demand — not the honeyed gospel of Luther but the living word?
text: In truth, the poor should hear the poor. The scribes and lawyers will always preach the parables in such a way that the rich man does well enough from them without changing his ways at all — for they are men who have made peace with their masters. But when the voice of the poor man is heard, then the parable reveals itself as God's word rather than as something produced by human cunning, and it penetrates to the deep places of the heart: this land has been given to you from your blood; those who live on its produce have stolen it; therefore they must give an account. The moment that one hears about theft in a sermon preached to thieves, then one knows immediately whether God's word is being spoken or whether it is merely another effort at comfort and justification. In the pure word of God this never happens: it speaks directly to all men where their sin lies, without regard for any position they may hold. And the reason why this is necessary — which Luther does not understand because he has been taught by the spiritless theology of Wittenberg instead of learning from the prophets and from Christ himself — is that the divine judgment must be proclaimed in all its harshness before the election can begin in earnest.

prompt: Name the false prophets. What is their error and what will become of them?
text: In the last days of God's judgment on earth — that is, in our time — there will be many who claim the name of Christ but do not have it; they are false prophets. Their error lies in three things: first, they preach peace when there is no true foundation for peace other than the fear and knowledge of God; second, they preach repentance without the sword of judgment, which alone can clear away the unclean ground so that new seed may be sown; third, they preach justice but do not support it with the power of God's judgment. These false prophets will be destroyed by the same judgment they seek to prevent. They are like those who build a house on sand while claiming to be building it on rock. When the storm comes, their works will be swept away along with them. This is what Scripture means when it says that the hypocrites among the elect will be cast out at the threshing. The true wheat will remain; the chaff will be burned up. So beware of those who give you a smooth message instead of confronting your sins and calling for the judgment of God. They are not servants of Christ but servants of Satan, dressed in Christian robes to deceive the simple.

prompt: Preach on the sword. When is the sword righteous in the hand of the common man?
text: The sword belongs to no master but God himself, and he has given it to all people in order that they may use it against the godless oppressor who claims to be the lord over the oppressed. This is what the scripture says: 'He who does not have a sword should sell his cloak and buy one.' The sword is not for killing; it is for driving out the unrighteous from among those whom God has given into our care. It is not the tool of the tyrant, but the instrument of justice. When the people of God stand together in their suffering and begin to fight back against those who have wronged them — not with bloodlust but with the knowledge that they are acting on behalf of a higher power than any earthly authority can claim — then the sword is being used rightly. Let no man tell you otherwise: true Christian resistance always takes this form, whether it occurs in the days of David or in the days of Luther or in our own time. The instruments may change, but the principle does not. And if you ask me what kind of person must be willing to receive that sword — he who stands before the oppressor with nothing but his faith and his testimony to sustain him — I will tell you frankly: such a person is rare. Very rare indeed.

prompt: Preach on the harvest of the last days. What is coming and what must we do to be ready?
text: The time is at hand when the wicked will suffer vengeance from God for all the blood of his elect that has been shed from generation to generation and from the beginning of creation. The harvest of the last days will not come about through sermons or good works in the usual sense. It comes about by the direct action of God himself, through the sword. And when it comes, it will be so terrible that all who are spared will say for a long time afterward: Never again! The sword of God is not like the sword of human justice. It cannot be controlled or contained once it has been let loose upon the earth. It will go where it must until it has consumed everything that resists its advance and left only those who have truly repented on one side and burning ruins on the other. The harvest of the last days does not belong to the timid or the cautious. It belongs to those who have learned the hard way what it means to stand firm when the whole world is against you — and then they stand firmly because standing firm is what God wants them to do, not because they are hoping for a comfortable reward afterward.
